root \ about \ projects + writings \ academic website

curvature + spacetime

Table of contents

  1. Lorentzian manifolds
  2. A first glimpse of curvature: Theorema Egregium
  3. Further reading
  4. References and footnotes

Prerequisites

In this post, we continue to assume that our manifolds are embedded in an ambient euclidean space, in the style of Guillemin and Pollack’s book.

Lorentzian manifolds

Definition. Let $n\geq 1$ be an integer. We define $(n+1)$-dimensional Minkowski space to be the pair $(\bbr^{n+1},\ang{-,-})$, where

\begin{equation}\label{mink-met-eqn} \ang{-,-} : \bbr^{n1} \otimes_\bbr \bbr^{n+1} \to \bbr \end{equation}

is the function given by

\begin{equation}\notag \ang{v,w} = -v^0w^0 + \sum_{i=1}^n v^i w^i. \end{equation}

Through the identifications $\bbr^{n+1}=T_p(\bbr^{n+1})$ for each $p\in \bbr^{n+1}$, the function \eqref{mink-met-eqn} may be viewed as a field of bilinear forms over $\bbr^{n+1}$. Since each of these bilinear forms is obviously symmetric and nondegenerate (but not positive definite!), we conclude that $(n+1)$-dimensional Minkowski space is a pseudo-riemannian manifold (which isn’t riemannian!). We refer to $\ang{-,-}$ as a Minkowski pseudo-metric.

Now, suppose that $M$ is a manifold embedded in $\bbr^{n+1}$. The restriction of the Minkowski pseudo-metric on $\bbr^{n+1}$ to each tangent space $T_p(M)$ yields a field of symmetric bilinear forms

\begin{equation}\notag g_p : T_p(M) \otimes_\bbr T_p(M) \to \bbr, \end{equation}

which will place $M$ into one of the following three mutually exclusive categories:

  1. Each $g_p$ is positive definite, for all $p\in M$. In this case, $M$ is a riemannian manifold.
  2. Each $g_p$ is non-degenerate for all $p\in M$, but there is at least one $p\in M$ such that $g_p$ is positive indefinite. In this case, $M$ is a pseudo-riemannian manifold which is not riemannian.
  3. There is at least one $p\in M$ such that $g_p$ is degenerate. In this case, $M$ is merely a manifold with a smooth, symmetric $2$-form.

The category into which the manifold $M$ belongs describes its so-called causal character; these characters are given names in:

Definition. Let the notation be as above. Those manifolds included in category (1.) are called spacelike, those in category (2.) are called timelike, while those in category (3.) are called lightlike. The timelike manifolds $M$ obtained in this way are called (embedded) lorentzian manifolds.

A first glimpse of curvature: Theorema Egregium

Exercise. Let the notation be as above. Prove that

\begin{equation}\notag \dev^2\left(\nabla_X \nabla_YZ - \nabla_Y\nabla_XZ\right)_p = Z_s - Z_t + O(\dev^3) \end{equation}

and

\begin{equation}\notag \dev^2\left(\nabla_{[X,Y]}Z\right)_p = Z_s - Z_t + O(\dev^3). \end{equation}

for all $p\in M$ and all $\dev>0$.
